cross_connections.display_label: Both Tunxis-related officials in early 1800s Farmington.
Revolutionary grandfather; she pioneered California through her marriage.
Both Hooker descendants involved with Tunxis affairs, 1738 and 1804.

military_service.notes: Battle of Long Island Jul 1776; Saratoga and SURRENDER OF BURGOYNE Oct 17, 1777; under Col. Noadiah Hooker 1779; Sergeant-Major 1779; clerk to Lt.-Col. Austin 1781. Maj.-Gen. Parsons recommended him to Washington for commission, May 20, 1782 (Library of Congress, Washington Papers, Vol.3, p.2122). Pensioned 1832.

Revolutionary War service per pension application: July 1776 under Capt. John Porter to NY (in Battle of Long Island, retreated to Harlem Plains and over King's Bridge to White Plains; regiment disbanded Sept 28, 1776); 1777 under Capt. Ichabod Norton (attacked fort at Kingsbridge, defeated, discharged); September 1777 joined Capt. Ichabod Norton commanding regt. of 4 cos. 15th Conn. Militia, proceeded to Saratoga, present at the decisive action of Oct 7 and at the surrender of Burgoyne Oct 17, 1777; 1779 under Capt. Elisha Scott Col. Noadiah Hooker to New Haven; served as Sergeant-Major under Major Ichabod Norton; September 1781 drafted for three-week tour to New Haven in Lt-Col. Aaron Austin's regt., taken into Col. Austin's family as clerk/secretary. Pensioned under Act of June 7, 1832 commencing March 4, 1831, paid to death August 4, 1850.

documents.document_blurb: 1804 Connecticut General Assembly Resolve appointing Ezekiel Cowles Jr. as Overseer of the Farmington Tunxis Indians (replacing Capt. John Porter).

career.title: Member, Connecticut State Legislature
career.notes: Represented Farmington in the State Legislature.

career.title: Overseer of the Tunxis Indians
career.notes: Appointed by the Connecticut Legislature in 1804 as overseer and advisor of the Tunxis (Farmington Indians), replacing Capt. John Porter (under whom Ezekiel had served in 1776). The position made him one of a series of state-appointed Anglo-American managers of what remained of the Tunxis people's affairs and lands during a period of dispossession.

career.title: Farmer
career.notes: Farmer at Farmington Connecticut throughout adult life.

narrative_blocks.header: Recommended to Washington for a commission
narrative_blocks.body: On May 20, 1782 Maj.-Gen. Samuel Holden Parsons wrote to General Washington recommending Sergeant-Major Ezekiel Cowles for a subaltern commission. Ezekiel had been at Long Island, Harlem Plains, and the surrender of Burgoyne; the war ended before the commission could be granted. The Parsons letter is preserved at the Library of Congress.

narrative_blocks.header: Appointed overseer of the Tunxis in 1804
narrative_blocks.body: In 1804 the Connecticut General Assembly appointed Ezekiel Cowles Jr. overseer and advisor of the Tunxis Indians at Farmington, replacing Capt. John Porter — under whom Ezekiel had marched to the Battle of Long Island in 1776. The role placed him in the same Tunxis-management chain as Justice John Mix, who signed the 1802 Sue Squa land deed and the 1816 Titus emancipation.

narrative_blocks.header: Four overlapping offices across twenty-five years
narrative_blocks.body: Ezekiel filled four overlapping public offices across twenty-five years in Farmington: State Tax Collector (1794-1798), Deputy Sheriff (1802-1814), State Legislator (1807-1810), and Justice of the Peace (1809-1818). His 1850 estate inventoried at $6,549.12 — a substantial farmer's wealth in mid-century Connecticut.

Ezekiel had been at Long Island, Harlem Plains, and the surrender of Burgoyne; the war ended before the commission could be granted. The Parsons letter is preserved at the Library of Congress.

narrative_blocks.body: In 1804 the Connecticut General Assembly appointed Ezekiel Cowles Jr. overseer and advisor of the Tunxis Indians at Farmington, replacing Capt. John Porter — under whom Ezekiel had marched to the Battle of Long Island in 1776. The role placed him in the same Tunxis-management chain as Justice John Mix, who signed the 1802 Sue Squa land deed and the 1816 Titus emancipation.

narrative_blocks.header: Four overlapping offices across twenty-five years
narrative_blocks.body: Ezekiel filled four overlapping public offices across twenty-five years in Farmington: State Tax Collector (1794-1798), Deputy Sheriff (1802-1814), State Legislator (1807-1810), and Justice of the Peace (1809-1818). His 1850 estate inventoried at $6,549.12 — a substantial farmer's wealth in mid-century Connecticut.

parents.father_research_notes: Samuel Woodruff of Farmington CT (Ensign Samuel³, Matthew², Matthew¹) per Cowles book #303. Not built per Jalapeño.
parents.mother_research_notes: Elizabeth Norton Woodruff of Farmington CT. Not built per Jalapeño.

sources.short: Cowles Families in America #678 — Egbert⁶ Cowles
Yenamesages of All Living New England Centenarians (c.1884) — Egbert Cowles biographical sketch at age 99

notable.notable_blurb: Connecticut state senator (1848), legislator, and probate judge

cross_connections.display_label: His son's father-in-law — Massachusetts disestablishment champion.

documents.document_blurb: Egbert Cowles's 1880 book 'Sketches of the Early Settlements of the Plymouth Colony' — written at age 95, self-taught.

institutions.role: Stockholder and supporter — Morning Star mission steamer (1884)
institutions.notes: Took stock in the Morning Star steamer; carved its axe helve.

military_service.notes: Quartermaster, 7th Connecticut Militia Cavalry, 1810-1812 (just prior to/at outbreak of War of 1812).

career.title: Farmer
career.notes: Large farmer in Farmington CT, living in the lower part of the town.

career.title: Member, Connecticut State Legislature
career.notes: Represented Farmington 1832.

career.title: Justice of the Peace
career.notes: 24-year tenure.

career.title: Judge of Probate Court
career.notes: Judge of the Probate Court for many years per Cowles book.

career.title: Author
career.notes: At age 95 published 'Sketches of the Early Settlements of the Plymouth Colony; Historical, Biographical and Anecdotical' (DOC019). Self-taught with limited formal schooling.

career.title: Congregational Church member
career.organization: First Church of Christ Congregational, Farmington CT
career.notes: Approximately 80 years of membership in the Farmington Congregational Church (joined ~1804 as a young adult). Urged establishment of a Sunday school at Scott's Swamp, an outer Farmington district.

career.title: American Board of Foreign Missions supporter
career.organization: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions (ABCFM)
career.notes: ABCFM was organized in Farmington in 1810 when Egbert was twenty-five. He took stock in the Morning Star mission steamer (launched ~1884 for Micronesian missionary work) and personally carved an axe helve for the cabin of the new vessel.

narrative_blocks.header: Society planned his hundredth birthday celebration
narrative_blocks.body: The Farmington Ecclesiastical Society arranged a celebration for Egbert Cowles's 100th birthday — but he died February 27, 1885, five weeks shy of the planned April 4 occasion. He had served as a Connecticut state senator, state legislator, deputy sheriff, justice of the peace for twenty-four years, and probate judge. 'A town or society meeting for a long series of years which did not listen to his addresses of wisdom was unknown.'

narrative_blocks.header: Five children died young, six survived
narrative_blocks.body: Egbert and Sophia had eleven children between 1813 and 1831 in Farmington Connecticut. Five died before age thirteen, with replacement-naming patterns cycling through Edgar, Arthur, and Lucius across multiple deceased siblings. Six survived to adulthood: Esther Rachel, Lucius Samuel, John Egbert, Alfred II, Arthur D., and Edgar Philip — including John Egbert who lived to meet Wilford Woodruff in Salt Lake City sixty years later.

narrative_blocks.header: Authored a Plymouth Colony history at ninety-five
narrative_blocks.body: At age 95 in 1880 — self-taught with only district-school education — Egbert published 'Sketches of the Early Settlements of the Plymouth Colony; Historical, Biographical and Anecdotical'. Contemporaries praised the book's clear concise style; Egbert had spent his hundred years in Farmington except for one Southern winter, two or three trips to Ohio, and a single journey to St. Louis.

narrative_blocks.header: Carved the Morning Star steamer's axe helve
narrative_blocks.body: Egbert was twenty-five when the American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions (ABCFM) was organized in Farmington in 1810; he remembered Governor Treadwell and Dr. Noah Porter framing the new society. Seventy-four years later in 1884 he took stock in the Morning Star mission steamer bound for the Micronesian Isles, and personally carved a beautiful axe helve for the new vessel's cabin.

burial.plot_notes: Memento Mori Cemetery Farmington Hartford County CT (FG cemetery #2098376). The historic Old Burying Ground also called Memento Mori. CEM not built — only confirmed Hooker connection so far is Egbert.

parents.father_research_notes: Samuel Woodruff of Southington CT (not built per Jalapeño). Cowles book gives Woodruff descent: Samuel → Samuel → Samuel → Samuel → Matthew. NOTE: Hinman attributed her father as 'Hon. Samuel Woodruff of Windsor CT, judge of the County Court, agent to Greece, published European travels' — Cowles book corrects this attribution to Samuel + Esther Sloper of Southington.
parents.mother_research_notes: Esther Sloper Woodruff of Southington CT (not built per Jalapeño).

narrative_blocks.header: Outlived six of her eleven children
narrative_blocks.body: Sophia bore eleven children with Egbert in Farmington Connecticut between 1813 and 1831. Five died before age thirteen, with replacement-naming patterns cycling through Edgar, Arthur, and Lucius across multiple deceased siblings; she also outlived a sixth child Arthur D. who died at twenty-nine in 1857. Sophia herself died at age 85 in August 1874, eleven years before Egbert reached his near-century.

sources.short: Cowles Families in America #678 — Egbert⁶ Cowles

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name 'Rachel' for her paternal grandmother Rachel Woodruff Cowles.

sources.short: Cowles Families in America #678 — Egbert⁶ Cowles
Cowles Families in America #678 — Egbert⁶ Cowles

narrative_blocks.header: Edgar died young
narrative_blocks.body: Edgar died in 1831 at the age of thirteen. His parents later gave the name to a younger brother.

burial.plot_notes: Riverside Cemetery, Farmington Hartford County CT. FG cemetery #103605. Single Hooker connection — CEM not built.

career.title: Merchant
career.notes: Merchant in Farmington CT until after 1850. Per Cowles book: in 1850 his cousin Augustus Cowles (#749) remembered him in his will 'for his kindness during my sickness.'

career.title: Wholesale dry goods merchant
career.notes: Wholesale dry goods merchant at Galena Illinois ~1850-1860.

career.title: Wholesale dry goods merchant
career.notes: Removed from Galena to Freeport Illinois in 1860; continued wholesale dry goods business.

military_service.notes: Captain commanding 1st Company, 14th Regiment, Connecticut Militia Infantry, 1842-43. An April 12, 1843 muster order in his hand is preserved in Julius Gay's account in the Farmington Magazine, July 1901: 'Attention! First Company, Fourteenth Regiment, Infantry: To either of the non-commissioned officers: You are hereby commanded to give legal warning to all the members of said Company to appear on parade at the usual place on Monday the first day of May next, at 9 o'clock A.M., armed and equipped as the law directs, for Company inspection and exercise.' He requested twelve rounds of blank cartridges.

narrative_blocks.header: Captain Cowles' 1843 Training Day muster order
narrative_blocks.body: Lucius Samuel served as Captain commanding 1st Company, 14th Regiment, Connecticut Militia Infantry from 1842-1843. An April 12, 1843 muster order in his own hand survives in Julius Gay's account in the Farmington Magazine for July 1901, ordering his company to parade May 1 'armed and equipped as the law directs' with twelve rounds of blank cartridges for company inspection.

narrative_blocks.header: From Farmington merchant to Galena wholesale
narrative_blocks.body: Lucius Samuel was a merchant in Farmington Connecticut until after 1850, then removed to Galena Illinois as a wholesale dry-goods merchant. He moved from Galena to Freeport Illinois in 1860, but both he and his wife Louise Sarah died back in Farmington — their names appear together on the Whitman family monument.

sources.short: Cowles Families in America #1685 — Capt. Lucius Samuel⁷ Cowles
Find a Grave — Capt. Lucius Samuel Cowles, Riverside Cemetery

notable.primary_url_label: Wilford Woodruff Papers — John Egbert Cowles

career.title: Traveling salesman
career.notes: Traveling salesman for a clothing store in New York City. Living in Farmington 1893 per Cowles book #1687.

naming_inspiration.description: Named for his father Egbert Cowles (H01138). Middle name 'Egbert' is the patronymic; 'John' is the personal name.

narrative_blocks.header: Met Wilford Woodruff in Salt Lake City, 1870
narrative_blocks.body: On January 24-25, 1870, John Egbert Cowles called on LDS apostle Wilford Woodruff at his Salt Lake City office. Woodruff — born in Farmington 1807 and away from Connecticut for over thirty years — wrote in his journal that John Egbert was 'the first man I have met with from my Native town for the last 20 years.' They spent hours discussing Farmington families, with John Egbert reporting that 'Nearly all of the families of the Cowles & Demings were dead & their property gone into other Hands.'

narrative_blocks.header: Distant cousin of the Mormon apostle
narrative_blocks.body: His mother Sophia Sarah Woodruff descended from Matthew Woodruff — the 1640 Farmington Connecticut proprietor — through five 'Samuel' generations. Wilford Woodruff likewise descended from Matthew via his grandfather Eldad and father Aphek. Likely the unspoken reason the apostle sought him out when he learned Cowles was visiting Salt Lake.

narrative_blocks.header: Founding families gone, property in other hands
narrative_blocks.body: Per Wilford Woodruff's journal of January 25, 1870: 'Nearly all of the families of the Cowles and Demings were dead, and their property gone into other hands.' Two centuries after Farmington's founding, two of its founding-era families had been substantially displaced — the news delivered to Woodruff by a fellow former Farmington resident across a Salt Lake desk.

narrative_blocks.header: Traveling salesman for a New York clothing store
narrative_blocks.body: Per the 1873 Cowles family genealogy entry #1687, he worked as 'a traveling salesman for a clothing store in N.Y. City; living in Farmington, 1893.' He kept his Connecticut residence through his commercial travels, dying in Farmington at eighty in 1905.

sources.short: Wilford Woodruff Papers — John Egbert Cowles entry
Cowles Families in America #1687 — John Egbert⁷ Cowles

documents.document_blurb: Wilford Woodruff Journal entries January 24-25, 1870 — meetings with John Egbert Cowles in Salt Lake City.

bio.bio_blurb: Hartford clerk wounded in the Overland Campaign

burial.plot_notes: Cowles-Wheeler Cemetery, East Willington Tolland County CT. FG cemetery #2147876. Multiple Cowles-Wheeler family burials including his wife Harriet (I00390).

career.title: Clerk
career.notes: Clerk in Hartford CT 1856.

career.title: Grocer
career.notes: Grocer in Hartford CT 1859.

career.notes: Bookkeeper in Hartford CT.

career.title: Post Office clerk
career.notes: Post Office clerk in Hartford CT.

career.title: Farmer
career.notes: Removed from Hartford to Willington CT

military_service.unit: Co. K, 14th Regt. Inf., Conn. Vols., 2d Army Corps, Army of the Potomac
military_service.notes: Enlisted as Private August 7, 1862. Wounded May 10, 1864 at the Battle of Laurel Hill, Virginia (during the Spotsylvania Court House campaign). Recovered and served until discharge September 7, 1865. The 14th Connecticut was part of the Army of the Potomac's 2d Army Corps and saw heavy action throughout the Overland Campaign.

naming_inspiration.description: Named for his paternal uncle Alfred Cowles (H01139), the lawyer who later migrated to California.

narrative_blocks.header: Wounded at Spotsylvania in the Overland Campaign
narrative_blocks.body: A Hartford clerk and grocer who enlisted in 1862, he took a wound at the Battle of Laurel Hill in May 1864 during the Spotsylvania fighting, then served to the war’s end. He finished his long life farming at Willington, dying at eighty-eight.

sources.short: Cowles Families in America #1688 — Alfred⁷ Cowles
Find a Grave — Alfred Cowles veteran, Cowles-Wheeler Cemetery
Civil War Service Records — 14th Connecticut Volunteer Infantry

bio.extra_names: Philip Edgar (Cowles book #1690); Philip Egbert (Town Records). EH 1909 #2332 has 'Edgar Philip' which this tree retains for display.

military_service.notes: Possibly served as private in Co. A, 25th Connecticut Volunteer Infantry, September 9, 1862 to August 26, 1863. Cowles book #1690 hedges with 'Perhaps' — service identification not fully confirmed; multiple Edgar/Philip Cowles men in CT records of that period.

narrative_blocks.header: Third Edgar in family — first two died young
narrative_blocks.body: Edgar Philip Cowles was the third son of Egbert and Sophia Cowles to bear the name Edgar — the first Edgar had died in infancy in 1817 and the second died in 1833 at age twelve to fourteen. This third Edgar survived to age sixty-five, dying June 29, 1896 in Farmington Connecticut. The replacement-naming pattern was common in early-Republic families memorializing children lost in epidemics, but rare to find three documented attempts before a name-bearer survived.

narrative_blocks.header: Name recorded three different ways across sources
narrative_blocks.body: His name appears in three different forms across the historical record: 'Edgar Philip,' 'Philip Edgar,' and 'Philip Egbert.' The 'Philip Egbert' variant may indicate that his father's patronymic Egbert was transcribed as Edgar by some sources; the Civil War service note similarly hedges 'perhaps he served as a private Company A, Twenty-fifth Connecticut Volunteer Infantry, September 1862 to August 1863.' The three-name puzzle remains unresolved.

sources.short: Cowles Families in America #1690 — Philip Edgar⁷ Cowles

notable.notable_blurb: Illinois lawyer who later migrated to California

cross_connections.display_label: Married his first cousin Charlotte from the parallel Cowles line.

career.title: Lawyer
career.notes: Practiced law in Illinois (Belleville per Cowles book — three children born there).

career.title: Lawyer
career.notes: Lawyer of note. Practiced first in Belleville Illinois (where his three eldest children were born 1817-1825), then migrated to California by 1884. Still practicing in San Diego at age 97 per Egbert's 1884 sketch.

narrative_blocks.header: Practicing law in San Diego at ninety-seven
narrative_blocks.body: Alfred Cowles studied law and migrated to Belleville Illinois with his cousin-wife Charlotte where their three eldest children were born 1817-1825. By 1884 he had moved to San Diego California and was still practicing 'as a lawyer of note' at age 97. His elder brother Egbert's 1884 biographical sketch records the fact, but Alfred's death year remains unconfirmed.

narrative_blocks.header: The books never recorded his death
narrative_blocks.body: In September 1816 he married his first cousin Charlotte Cowles; the family lawyered in Belleville, Illinois, then followed two daughters to California. Neither the Hooker genealogy nor the Cowles book ever learned when he died — last seen “a lawyer of note” in San Diego, past ninety-seven.

parents.mother_research_notes: Polly Gleason Cowles of Farmington CT (not built per Jalapeño). Wife of Capt. Solomon Cowles X01975.

cross_connections.display_label: Married her first cousin Alfred from the Hooker-line Cowles branch.

naming_inspiration.description: Named for his uncle Frederick Woodruff Cowles (H01140) who died at 18 months before this Frederick was born.

burial.plot_notes: Mount Hope Cemetery San Diego CA per Cowles book #1695. CEM not built — single Hooker connection.

education.notes: Educated at Willoughby Female Seminary, Lake County Ohio.

cross_connections.display_label: Pioneered California while her grandfather served the Revolution.
Distant Hooker cousins, both touched by the Civil War.

narrative_blocks.header: Belleville Illinois to Ensenada Baja California
narrative_blocks.body: Cornelia was born in Belleville Illinois in 1829, where her father Alfred practiced law. Her family migrated to California in 1853, and she married Mexican-War-era medical officer Erasmus Darwin French in San Jose January 1858. By 1894 the couple resided in Ensenada Baja California Mexico — three thousand miles from her father's Connecticut birthplace.

education.notes: Educated at Willoughby Female Seminary, Lake County Ohio.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name 'Gleason' for her maternal grandmother Polly Gleason Cowles.

narrative_blocks.header: Willoughby Seminary then a Nevada City marriage
narrative_blocks.body: Elizabeth was born 1833 in Belleville Illinois and educated at Willoughby Female Seminary in Lake County Ohio (the same school as her elder sister Cornelia). She married enterprising Nevada City California merchant Charles Carroll Leavitt at her parents' San Jose home; the family later settled in Oakland while Leavitt served in the San Francisco Custom House for many years.

naming_inspiration.description: Named for both father (Alfred Cowles H01139) and paternal grandfather (Ezekiel Cowles Jr. H00439).

sources.short: Edward Hooker 1909 — entry #1140 Frederick Woodruff Cowles
Edward Hooker 1909 — entry #1140a Ebenezer Mix Cowles

career.title: Merchant
career.notes: Merchant at Milledgeville Georgia per EH #1140a.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost his Southern fortune in the Civil War
narrative_blocks.body: Ebenezer had emigrated from Farmington, Connecticut to Milledgeville, Georgia before 1843 — Georgia's state capital and center of antebellum planter society. By 1860 the family had moved to Huntsville, Alabama. The Civil War erased everything he had built in the South, leaving his widow to support herself and their children alone.

narrative_blocks.header: His daughter and son-in-law anchored Atlanta's rail economy
narrative_blocks.body: His eldest daughter Florence married Edward A. Werner, a pioneer Atlanta citizen and Georgia Railroad agent who had arrived in Atlanta in 1845. His son Clifford Atwood got his first railroad job as a messenger in Werner's office in 1874, climbing through railroad management positions across the South for the next two decades.

narrative_blocks.header: Connecticut native who chose the cotton South
narrative_blocks.body: Ebenezer's 1840s move from Farmington, Connecticut to Milledgeville — Georgia's antebellum state capital — followed a pattern of Northern professionals drawn to the cotton-and-rail economy emerging in the Deep South. The choice ran counter to other branches of the Cowles family: his abolitionist Cleveland kinsmen worked the Underground Railroad while he built a life in slaveholding Georgia. The Civil War ended his Southern enterprise and the inheritance it might have left.

narrative_blocks.header: Her Atwood family gave her children Mayflower descent
narrative_blocks.body: Frances's maiden name Atwood gave her son Clifford his middle name and the family its Mayflower Descendants membership — he was ninth in descent from Pilgrim Richard Warren. She is the ancestral source of the Cowles-Atwood Mayflower thread that runs through Florence, Irene, and Clifford's lines.

narrative_blocks.header: Raised three children alone after the Civil War
narrative_blocks.body: When the Civil War stripped Ebenezer of everything he had built in Georgia and Alabama, Frances was left to support herself and their children. Her eldest daughter Florence's marriage to Georgia Railroad agent Edward Werner in 1872 became the family's economic anchor in the post-war years.

sources.short: Edward Hooker 1909 — entry #1140a Ebenezer Mix Cowles

burial.plot_notes: Oakland Cemetery, Atlanta — with husband Edward and all four children

education.notes: Per EH: 'educated at the Huntsville Female Seminary, Huntsville, Ala., a Presbyterian institution, which at that time was foremost in educating young ladies.'

institutions.notes: Per EH 1909, attended Huntsville Female Seminary.

narrative_blocks.header: Born in Georgia's capital, educated in Alabama
narrative_blocks.body: Florence was born March 6, 1843 in Milledgeville — Georgia's state capital — and educated at the Huntsville Female Seminary, a Presbyterian institution then foremost in educating young Southern women. She married Georgia Railroad agent Edward Werner in Huntsville in December 1872 and settled permanently in Atlanta.

narrative_blocks.header: Her estate sparked a Georgia Supreme Court battle
narrative_blocks.body: Florence died November 29, 1911, never having distributed her late husband Edward's estate to their four children. Her son Lynn assumed management without court authority; by 1943 the estate dispute reached the Georgia Supreme Court, where Edward Jr. sued Lynn, Florence, and Ray over property mismanagement and a disputed sale of 528 West Peachtree Street Atlanta.

sources.short: Werner v. Werner, 195 Ga. 868 (Ga. Sup. Ct. May 6, 1943, Reid CJ)

education.notes: Per EH: same institution as sister Florence.

institutions.notes: Per EH 1909, attended Huntsville Female Seminary.

education.notes: Per EH: 'Mr. Cowles received only a common school education, as his father lost his all by the Civil War, and after his death it became a struggle for his widow to support herself.'

career.title: Messenger boy in Georgia Railroad office
career.notes: Brother-in-law Edward Werner 'took an interest in him, and in 1874 provided a position for him as messenger boy in his office.' Learned telegraphy and stenography in this period. Left 1881.

career.title: Clerk to superintendent
career.organization: Alabama, Great Southern Railroad, Chattanooga TN
career.notes: Accepted position with Alabama, Great Southern Railroad at Chattanooga as clerk to superintendent.

career.title: Clerk to superintendent
career.organization: East Tennessee, Virginia & Georgia Railway, Atlanta
career.notes: Returned to Atlanta 1882. Clerk to superintendent; then chief clerk; Nov 1887 to contracting freight agent at Chattanooga.

career.title: Contracting freight agent
career.notes: Jacksonville FL (Aug 1888), Atlanta (Nov 1888).

career.title: General Southern Traveling Agent
career.notes: 'In August, 1889, he was offered and accepted the position of Gen. So. Traveling Agent, Atlanta, Ga.' (EH)

narrative_blocks.header: Georgia Railroad messenger boy risen to traveling agent
narrative_blocks.body: When his brother-in-law Edward Werner gave Clifford his first railroad job as a messenger boy in 1874, he began a fifteen-year climb through Southern railroad management. By 1889 — two decades after his father lost everything in the Civil War — Clifford was General Southern Traveling Agent for a major rail line, based in Atlanta.

narrative_blocks.header: Named Atwood for his mother's Mayflower-descent family
narrative_blocks.body: Clifford was named for his mother's family: Frances M. Atwood Cowles carried Mayflower descent ninth from Pilgrim Richard Warren (1580-1628). Clifford was a member of the Massachusetts Society of Mayflower Descendants — his 'Atwood' middle name and that membership are both direct expressions of his mother's lineage.

sources.short: Massachusetts Society of Mayflower Descendants — Clifford Atwood Cowles, ninth from Richard Warren
Congressional Directory (re: father-in-law Col. Milton Anthony Candler, 44th–45th Congress 1875-1879)
Genealogy of the Cowles family in America (Cowles, 1929)
Memorial History of Hartford County, Connecticut (Trumbull ed., 1886) — Unionville chapter by James L. Cowles

career.title: Selectman of Farmington
career.notes: Documented as Selectman of Farmington on January 10, 1816 when he co-signed the certificate of examination for the manumission of Titus, slave of Capt. Joshua Youngs (alongside Justice of the Peace John Mix and fellow Selectman Samuel Richards). Per Trumbull's Memorial History of Hartford County (1886), Unionville chapter by James L. Cowles.

cross_connections.display_label: Her second cousin Abigail married her husband's younger brother James.

sources.short: Connecticut Town Marriage Records pre-1870 (Barbour Collection)

parents.father_research_notes: Samuel Cowles 1639-1691 of Farmington CT (not built). Per WikiTree.
parents.mother_research_notes: Abigail Stanley Cowles 1637-1734 of Farmington CT (not built). Per WikiTree.

narrative_blocks.header: Married two Andrews sisters in succession
narrative_blocks.body: His first wife Mary Andrews died July 1705; four years later in November 1709 he married her sister Sarah Andrews. Sarah herself died June 1715 leaving only one infant who had died in 1714 — Isaac married for the third and last time in December 1716 to Elizabeth Smith, twenty-six years his junior.

narrative_blocks.header: Farmington tailor who became Captain by 1731
narrative_blocks.body: A tailor by trade and extensive landholder at Farmington, he rose through the militia ranks — Ensign 1717, Lieutenant 1723, Captain by May 1731 of the 2nd Company Trainband. He also served as Company Clerk in 1714, collector of the minister's rate 1698-1700, constable for several years, and town representative in 1726.

narrative_blocks.header: Five surviving sons divided 2500 pounds, 1763
narrative_blocks.body: His will was dated October 1740 and proved March 1756. Seven years later in 1763 his last five surviving sons divided his Farmington estate — approximately £2,500 among themselves as sole heirs, an unusually concentrated inheritance for the colonial period.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: His father lived here, directly opposite the Giles Hooker house on Main Street.

narrative_blocks.header: His death at 96 killed his brother Samuel
narrative_blocks.body: On February 19, 1787, Giles Hooker died in Farmington at age 96. When the news reached his older brother Samuel — then age 98 in Kensington — Samuel "fell back in his chair and expired" on March 1, 1787. Two brothers, together accounting for nearly 195 years of life, died within ten days of each other.

narrative_blocks.header: The well stone on Main Street
narrative_blocks.body: He and Martha lived on the George Cowles place on Main Street, Farmington. In Edward Hooker's time (1909), a flat stone in the sidewalk just below the brow of the hill still covered the old well of the Giles Hooker house. Directly opposite, his father Rev. Samuel Hooker had lived in the Mix House — just south of the old burying ground.

narrative_blocks.header: Her grandfather was Secretary of State
narrative_blocks.body: Her maternal grandfather was Hon. John Allyn (Allen), Secretary of State for the Colony of Connecticut. Her father, Capt. Aaron Cooke IV, served as Hartford's deputy to the Connecticut General Assembly for fifteen sessions and chaired the committee to distribute the new laws in 1702.

crossConnections.display_label: Both Tunxis-related officials in early 1800s Farmington.
Revolutionary grandfather; she pioneered California through her marriage.
Both Hooker descendants involved with Tunxis affairs, 1738 and 1804.
